serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of cooked rice in Singapore is approximately 200g, equivalent to roughly one cup of steamed rice as typically served at hawker centres or home meals.

nutrient_source.title: Rice, cooked — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for cooked rice (G00010081). The nutrient profile — particularly elevated dietary fibre (3.7g/100g), sodium (231mg/100g), potassium (327mg/100g), and fat (4.21g/100g) — is atypical for plain boiled white rice and may reflect a mixed rice dish or enriched preparation. Values are preserved as reported by HPB with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 is based on white rice (cooked, boiled) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). The HPB nutrient profile for this record is atypical for plain white rice; however, GI 72 is applied as the best available approved-source estimate for a rice-based dish. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 50.38) / 100 ≈ 36.3 for a 200g serving (25.19g carbohydrate per 100g × 2 = 50.38g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231mg/100g with partial offset from potassium 327mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 231mg/100g is elevated for a plain rice dish and warrants portion control; the potassium-to-sodium ratio of approximately 1.4:1 provides partial DASH-diet benefit, but individuals with hypertension should limit serving size to one bowl (200g) and avoid adding salt or high-sodium condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) with 25.2g carbohydrate per 100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: White rice carries a high GI of approximately 72, producing a glycemic load of ~36 per standard 200g bowl; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, prefer brown or mixed-grain rice for lower GI, and pair rice with high-fibre vegetables and lean protein to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (231mg/100g) and moderate fat (4.21g/100g) relative to plain rice norms
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The sodium content of 231mg/100g exceeds typical plain boiled rice levels and may contribute to cardiovascular risk if consumed in large portions; total fat at 4.21g/100g is modest but saturated fat breakdown is unavailable, so individuals with CVD should monitor overall dietary sodium and fat intake and favour brown or whole-grain rice preparations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Neutral staple; dietary fibre 3.7g/100g supports gut microbiome and immune modulation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Rice is a neutral, non-inflammatory staple; dietary fibre at 3.7g/100g supports gut microbiome health relevant to immune modulation in rheumatoid arthritis, but plain white rice in excess may contribute to glycaemic load-driven systemic inflammation — prefer brown or mixed-grain rice and pair with anti-inflammatory accompaniments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rice is a cooked grain staple that forms the dietary foundation of Singaporean and broader Asian cuisine. In Singapore, steamed or boiled white rice is served at virtually every hawker centre, coffee shop, and home meal, typically accompanying meat, fish, vegetable, and tofu dishes. The HPB nutrient profile for this record (G00010081) reports 164 kcal, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, and 25.19g carbohydrate per 100g, with notably elevated dietary fibre (3.7g/100g), sodium (231mg/100g), potassium (327mg/100g), calcium (54mg/100g), and iron (2.55mg/100g) compared to plain boiled white rice norms — suggesting this profile may represent a mixed rice dish or an enriched preparation rather than plain white rice. Texture is soft and slightly sticky when freshly cooked; flavour is mild and neutral, making it an ideal base for strongly flavoured accompaniments. Clinically, white rice carries a high glycemic index (~72) and a glycemic load of approximately 36 per standard 200g bowl, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium at 231mg/100g is borderline for hypertension management. Potassium at 327mg/100g and fibre at 3.7g/100g provide partial cardiovascular and metabolic benefit. Rice is naturally gluten-free, halal, vegan, and suitable for most dietary patterns when consumed in appropriate portions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of rice?
What is the GI of white rice?
How much sodium is in cooked rice?
Can I eat rice if I have high blood pressure?
What is the carbohydrate content of rice?
Is brown rice healthier than white rice?
How much rice should I eat per meal?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010081) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables for white rice. Multilingual names verified against standard Singaporean language usage.
notes: 1. The HPB nutrient profile for this record is atypical for plain boiled white rice: dietary fibre at 3.7g/100g, sodium at 231mg/100g, potassium at 327mg/100g, calcium at 54mg/100g, iron at 2.55mg/100g, and fat at 4.21g/100g all exceed typical plain white rice values. This profile likely reflects a mixed rice dish or enriched preparation. Values are preserved as reported by HPB. 2. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this HPB rice record is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026-04-16. 3. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported in the HPB source record for this entry; not available in HPB Foodo database for this specific dish code as of 2026-04-16. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; rice is a plant-based grain and naturally contains no cholesterol. 5. GI of 72 applied as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for white rice (cooked, boiled); this record may represent a mixed rice dish with potentially different GI characteristics. 6. Glycemic load calculated for a 200g serving: (72 × 50.38) / 100 = 36.3.
